items.title: PyHS3 Benchmarks
items.text:
Welcome to the documentation for PyHS3 Benchmarks, the benchmarking and validation suite for the PyHS3 ecosystem.

This project provides a reproducible framework for evaluating every major stage of the statistical inference workflow, from loading serialized HS3 workspaces to executing compiled likelihood evaluations. In addition to profiling PyHS3 itself, the repository includes cross-framework comparisons with RooFit, xRooFit, and pyhf to validate numerical consistency and measure relative performance.

The benchmarking suite is designed for both development and research:

  • evaluate performance of individual workflow stages;
  • identify computational bottlenecks;
  • measure memory usage and scalability;
  • compare different statistical frameworks;
  • validate numerical agreement between implementations;
  • detect performance regressions over time.

items.title: Documentation
items.text: The documentation is organized by topic.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
If you are new to the project, begin here.

  • Getting Started
  • Installation
  • Repository Structure

items.title: Benchmarking
items.text:
Documentation for the PyHS3 workflow benchmarks.

These benchmarks measure the cost of each major stage of the statistical model lifecycle, including workspace loading, model creation, graph optimization, compilation, likelihood evaluation, and scaling.

Topics include:

  • Workspace Loading
  • Model Creation
  • Log-Probability Construction
  • Log-Probability Compilation
  • Graph Canonicalization
  • Graph Optimization
  • Compiled Evaluation
  • PDF Evaluation
  • NLL Scan
  • Memory Scaling
  • Model Complexity Scaling

items.title: Cross-Framework Comparisons
items.text:
Cross-framework benchmarks evaluate both numerical agreement and runtime performance across multiple statistical toolkits.

Current comparisons include:

  • PyHS3 vs RooFit
  • PyHS3 vs xRooFit
  • Scalar PDF Evaluation
  • Vectorized PDF Evaluation
  • Binned Likelihood Evaluation
  • NLL Scan
  • Model Complexity Scaling

items.title: Workspaces
items.text:
This section describes the benchmark inputs used throughout the project.

Topics include:

  • Alexx benchmark workspaces
  • Synthetic scalar PDF workspaces
  • Synthetic binned likelihood models
  • ROOT workspaces
  • Workspace generation

items.title: Outputs
items.text:
Benchmark execution produces structured JSON reports together with publication-quality plots.

The documentation explains:

  • benchmark outputs;
  • directory layout;
  • generated figures;
  • summary reports;
  • benchmark overview plots.

items.title: Development
items.text:
Information for contributors.

Topics include:

  • adding new benchmarks;
  • registering benchmarks;
  • extending the matrix runner;
  • benchmarking conventions;
  • validation guidelines.

items.title: Benchmark Workflow
items.text:
The benchmarking pipeline follows the complete lifecycle of an HS3 statistical model.

HS3 Workspace
      │
      ▼
Workspace Loading
      │
      ▼
Model Creation
      │
      ▼
Log-Probability Construction
      │
      ▼
Graph Canonicalization
      │
      ▼
Graph Optimization
      │
      ▼
Compilation
      │
      ▼
Compiled Evaluation
      │
      ├──────────────► PDF Evaluation
      │
      ├──────────────► NLL Scan
      │
      ├──────────────► Memory Profiling
      │
      └──────────────► Cross-Framework Benchmarks

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
Install the project:

pixi install

Run a benchmark:

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks workspace_loading

Generate the benchmark overview:

pixi run python -m src.plot_benchmark_overview \
    --results-dir results/alexx_full_matrix \
    --plot-dir plots/alexx_full_matrix/benchmark_overview

For detailed instructions, continue with the Getting Started guide.

items.title: Benchmark Methodology
items.text:
This document describes the benchmarking methodology used throughout the PyHS3 Benchmarks project.

Although individual benchmarks measure different stages of the statistical inference workflow, they all follow the same execution model and reporting conventions. Using a unified methodology ensures that benchmark results are reproducible, comparable, and suitable for long-term performance tracking.

items.title: Design Principles
items.text: Every benchmark in this repository follows four guiding principles.

items.title: Isolation
items.text:
Each benchmark measures a single stage of the statistical workflow whenever possible.

For example:

  • Workspace Loading measures only workspace deserialization.
  • Model Creation measures model construction independently of workspace loading.
  • Compiled Evaluation measures numerical execution independently of compilation.
  • Memory Scaling isolates memory consumption during selected workflow stages.

Isolating benchmark stages makes it significantly easier to identify performance bottlenecks.

items.title: Reproducibility
items.text:
All benchmark inputs are deterministic.

Workspace generators use fixed random seeds where applicable, ensuring that identical benchmark configurations always produce identical benchmark inputs.

As a result, performance differences between benchmark runs are primarily caused by implementation changes rather than input variability.

items.title: Automation
items.text:
All benchmark suites are designed to execute automatically.

The repository provides a unified matrix runner capable of

  • discovering benchmark workspaces;
  • executing benchmark suites;
  • collecting benchmark outputs;
  • organizing benchmark results;
  • generating benchmark summaries.

This allows complete benchmark campaigns to be executed using a single command.

items.title: Extensibility
items.text:
Benchmark implementations share a common execution model.

Adding a new benchmark typically requires only

  • implementing the benchmark itself;
  • registering it in the matrix runner;
  • documenting the benchmark.

Existing reporting and plotting infrastructure can then be reused without modification.

items.title: Benchmark Lifecycle
items.text:
Every benchmark follows the same high-level execution pipeline.

Input Workspace
        │
        ▼
Load Benchmark Input
        │
        ▼
Prepare Benchmark State
        │
        ▼
Warm-up (optional)
        │
        ▼
Repeated Measurements
        │
        ▼
Result Aggregation
        │
        ▼
JSON Report
        │
        ▼
Plot Generation

Each stage is described below.

items.title: Warm-up
items.text:
Some benchmarks perform one or more warm-up iterations before collecting measurements.

Warm-up executions are not included in the reported statistics.

Their purpose is to eliminate one-time initialization costs such as

  • JIT compilation;
  • graph initialization;
  • memory allocation;
  • cache population.

This produces more representative timing measurements for repeated execution.

items.title: Repeated Measurements
items.text:
Timing benchmarks are executed multiple times.

Repeated execution reduces measurement noise introduced by

  • operating system scheduling;
  • background processes;
  • cache effects;
  • temporary resource contention.

Most timing benchmarks therefore report aggregated statistics rather than a single execution time.

items.title: Numerical Validation
items.text:
Several benchmark suites compare multiple statistical frameworks.

These comparisons measure both

  • execution performance;
  • numerical agreement.

Validation ensures that benchmark optimizations do not change the underlying statistical model.

Depending on the benchmark, validation may compare

  • PDF values;
  • negative log-likelihood values;
  • likelihood scan shapes;
  • compiled versus interpreted execution.

items.title: Benchmark Outputs
items.text:
Each benchmark produces a structured JSON report containing

  • benchmark metadata;
  • benchmark configuration;
  • execution status;
  • timing statistics;
  • memory statistics (when applicable);
  • validation results (when applicable).

JSON outputs provide a stable interface for downstream visualization and automated analysis.

items.title: Plot Generation
items.text:
Most benchmark suites optionally generate publication-quality figures.

Typical visualizations include

  • execution time comparisons;
  • scaling behaviour;
  • memory consumption;
  • framework comparisons;
  • benchmark summaries.

Plots are generated from benchmark outputs and therefore can be recreated without rerunning the benchmark itself.

items.title: Matrix Benchmarking
items.text:
Large benchmark campaigns are executed using the matrix runner.

Rather than running a benchmark on a single workspace, the matrix runner executes the same benchmark over an entire collection of workspaces while preserving a consistent directory structure for all outputs.

This approach enables systematic evaluation across models of different complexity without requiring manual orchestration.

items.title: Performance Metrics
items.text:
Different benchmark suites report different performance metrics.

Common metrics include

Metric Description Wall time Total execution time measured by the benchmark Mean execution time Average over repeated runs Standard deviation Variability between repeated executions Peak memory Maximum resident memory usage Validation status Numerical agreement across implementations

Each benchmark page documents any additional benchmark-specific metrics.

items.title: Reproducibility Recommendations
items.text:
For consistent benchmark results, it is recommended to

  • use identical benchmark inputs;
  • execute benchmarks on an otherwise idle system;
  • avoid mixing debug and optimized builds;
  • compare results generated with identical benchmark configurations.

Following these recommendations minimizes measurement variability and improves comparability between benchmark campaigns.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
This guide introduces the basic workflow for working with the PyHS3 Benchmarks repository.

By the end of this guide you will be able to

  • install the project;
  • execute benchmark suites;
  • understand the generated outputs;
  • generate benchmark plots;
  • navigate the repository documentation.

If you have not yet installed the project, see the Installation guide before continuing.

items.title: Benchmark Workflow
items.text:
A typical benchmark session consists of four steps.

Install Dependencies
        │
        ▼
Run Benchmarks
        │
        ▼
Inspect Results
        │
        ▼
Generate Plots

The repository automates this workflow through the matrix runner and the plotting utilities.

items.title: Running Your First Benchmark
items.text:
The recommended way to execute benchmarks is through the matrix runner.

For example,

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks workspace_loading

This command automatically

  • discovers benchmark workspaces;
  • executes the selected benchmark;
  • stores benchmark reports;
  • generates the standard output directory structure.

For a description of the available benchmark suites, see the Benchmark Suite documentation.

items.title: Running Multiple Benchmarks
items.text:
Several benchmark suites can be executed during a single run.

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks \
        workspace_loading \
        model_creation \
        compiled_evaluation

To execute every supported benchmark,

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks all

items.title: Benchmark Outputs
items.text:
Benchmark execution produces structured JSON reports stored in the results/ directory.

A typical output hierarchy looks like

results/

    workspace_loading/

        10ch_bkgGenExp_sigGeneric_shapeFloat_npOn_constrGauss_yield1x/

            repeat_000/

                workspace_loading_result.json

See Benchmark Results for a detailed description of the output format.

items.title: Generating Plots
items.text:
Most benchmarks can optionally generate plots during execution.

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks workspace_loading \
    --plot

Generated figures are written to the plots/ directory.

items.title: Benchmark Overview
items.text:
After executing one or more benchmark suites, an overview report can be generated.

pixi run python -m src.plot_benchmark_overview \
    --results-dir results/alexx_workspace_matrix \
    --plot-dir plots/alexx_workspace_matrix/benchmark_overview

The overview aggregates benchmark reports into publication-quality summary figures covering

  • execution time;
  • memory usage;
  • benchmark status.

items.title: Typical Development Workflow
items.text:
The recommended workflow when developing or modifying benchmarks is

Modify Benchmark
        │
        ▼
Execute Matrix Runner
        │
        ▼
Inspect JSON Reports
        │
        ▼
Generate Overview Plots
        │
        ▼
Verify Results

Using the same workflow throughout the repository ensures that benchmark outputs remain consistent and directly comparable.

items.title: Where to Go Next
items.text:
The documentation is organized to support both new users and contributors.

If you are new to the repository, continue with

  • Installation
  • Repository Structure
  • Benchmark Methodology

If you want to understand the benchmark implementations, continue with

  • Benchmark Workspaces
  • Benchmark Suite
  • Cross-Framework Benchmarks

items.title: Installation
items.text: This guide describes how to install the PyHS3 Benchmarks repository and verify that the benchmarking environment is configured correctly.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Before installing the project, ensure that the following software is available.

  • Git
  • Pixi
  • Python (managed by Pixi)

The repository relies on Pixi to create a reproducible development environment with all required dependencies.

items.title: Clone the Repository
items.text:
Clone the repository from GitHub.

git clone https://github.com/scipp-atlas/pyhs3-benchmarks.git

cd pyhs3-benchmarks

items.title: Install Dependencies
items.text:
Create the project environment using Pixi.

pixi install

Pixi automatically installs all required dependencies and creates an isolated environment for benchmark execution.

items.title: Verify the Installation
items.text:
To verify that the installation completed successfully, execute a simple benchmark.

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks workspace_loading \
    --limit 1

If the benchmark completes successfully, the installation is ready for use.

items.title: Verify Plot Generation
items.text:
Benchmark plots can be generated using

pixi run python -m src.plot_benchmark_overview \
    --results-dir results/alexx_workspace_matrix \
    --plot-dir plots/alexx_workspace_matrix/benchmark_overview

Successful execution confirms that both the benchmarking framework and plotting utilities are functioning correctly.

items.title: Updating the Environment
items.text:
If project dependencies change, update the local environment using

pixi install

Pixi automatically synchronizes the environment with the project configuration.

items.title: Pixi is not installed
items.text: Install Pixi by following the official installation instructions.

items.title: Missing dependencies
items.text:
Run

pixi install

again to ensure that all required packages are installed.

items.title: Benchmark execution fails
items.text:
Verify that

  • the benchmark workspace directory exists;
  • the selected benchmark name is valid;
  • the project environment was created successfully.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
After completing the installation, continue with

  • Getting Started
  • Repository Structure
  • Benchmark Methodology

items.title: Benchmark Results
items.text:
Every benchmark execution produces structured outputs that can be used for analysis, visualization, validation, and long-term performance tracking.

All benchmark outputs are deterministic and organized using a consistent directory structure, allowing large benchmark campaigns to be reproduced and compared across repository revisions.

items.title: Output Directories
items.text:
Benchmark outputs are organized into two primary directories.

results/
plots/

The results directory stores structured benchmark reports, while plots contains generated visualizations.

Keeping numerical results separate from figures allows plots to be regenerated without rerunning benchmarks.

items.title: Results Directory
items.text:
The matrix runner stores benchmark outputs using the following hierarchy.

results/

    <benchmark>/

        <workspace>/

            repeat_000/

                <benchmark>_result.json

            repeat_001/

                <benchmark>_result.json

            ...

        ...

    matrix_summary.json

This structure scales naturally from a single benchmark execution to large benchmark campaigns involving dozens of workspaces.

Each benchmark execution is completely independent from every other execution.

items.title: Benchmark Reports
items.text:
Each benchmark generates a JSON report describing the execution.

Although the exact contents depend on the benchmark, reports typically contain

  • benchmark metadata;
  • benchmark configuration;
  • workspace information;
  • execution status;
  • timing statistics;
  • memory statistics;
  • validation results;
  • benchmark-specific measurements.

These JSON files serve as the primary machine-readable output of the repository.

items.title: Matrix Summary
items.text:
After completing a benchmark campaign, the matrix runner generates

matrix_summary.json

This file summarizes the entire execution.

Typical information includes

  • executed benchmarks;
  • processed workspaces;
  • execution status;
  • failed runs;
  • benchmark locations.

The summary provides a convenient entry point for automated analysis and reporting.

items.title: Generated Figures
items.text:
Plots generated by the benchmark framework are written to

plots/

using a directory hierarchy mirroring the corresponding benchmark results.

Typical figures include

  • execution time comparisons;
  • memory usage;
  • scaling behaviour;
  • framework comparisons;
  • likelihood scans;
  • benchmark overview plots.

Since plots are generated from benchmark reports, they can be recreated at any time without repeating benchmark execution.

items.title: Benchmark Overview
items.text:
The repository includes a dedicated overview generator that summarizes complete benchmark campaigns.

Rather than visualizing a single benchmark, the overview aggregates results across multiple benchmark suites and presents them using consistent reporting conventions.

Typical overview plots include

  • performance summaries;
  • timing comparisons;
  • memory summaries.

These reports provide a high-level view of benchmark performance across the entire repository.

items.title: Repeated Executions
items.text:
Many benchmarks execute multiple repeated measurements.

Each repetition is stored independently.

repeat_000/

repeat_001/

repeat_002/

Storing repetitions separately preserves the complete benchmark history and simplifies debugging when individual executions fail.

items.title: Benchmark Status
items.text:
Every benchmark execution records its completion status.

Typical values include

  • success
  • failed
  • timeout
  • error
  • skipped

Status information allows automated tools to distinguish successful benchmark results from incomplete executions.

items.title: Reproducibility
items.text:
Benchmark outputs are intended to be reproducible.

Running the same benchmark configuration on identical benchmark inputs should produce equivalent numerical results, while execution times may vary slightly depending on the hardware and execution environment.

For this reason, benchmark reports always include the benchmark configuration together with the measured statistics.

items.title: Typical Workflow
items.text:
A typical benchmark workflow looks as follows.

Benchmark
     │
     ▼
JSON Report
     │
     ▼
Matrix Summary
     │
     ▼
Overview Builder
     │
     ▼
Plots
     │
     ▼
Documentation

This workflow separates benchmark execution from visualization, making it possible to regenerate figures and reports without repeating expensive benchmark runs.

items.title: Using Benchmark Results
items.text:
Benchmark reports are intended to support several use cases.

  • performance regression detection;
  • optimization studies;
  • framework comparisons;
  • scalability analysis;
  • publication-quality figures;
  • automated reporting.

Because every benchmark follows the same reporting conventions, analysis tools can operate uniformly across the repository without requiring benchmark-specific logic.

items.title: Repository Structure
items.text:
This document describes the overall organization of the PyHS3 Benchmarks repository and explains the role of each major component.

The project is organized around a simple principle:

  • inputs define benchmark data;
  • benchmarks perform measurements;
  • results store benchmark outputs;
  • plots visualize benchmark results;
  • documentation describes the benchmarking framework.

items.title: Repository Layout
items.text:
pyhs3-benchmarks/
│
├── docs/
├── src/
├── inputs/
├── results/
├── plots/
├── profiling/
├── tests/
│
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml
└── pixi.toml

Each directory serves a specific purpose and is described below.

items.title: Source Code
items.text:
src/

The src directory contains the benchmarking implementation.

It includes

  • workflow benchmarks;
  • cross-framework benchmarks;
  • plotting utilities;
  • workspace generators;
  • shared benchmarking utilities.

Most users interact with the repository exclusively through the command-line interfaces provided by these modules.

items.title: Benchmark Inputs
items.text:
inputs/

The inputs directory stores benchmark datasets and statistical workspaces.

Depending on the benchmark, these include

  • Alexx benchmark workspaces;
  • synthetic scalar PDF workspaces;
  • synthetic binned likelihood models;
  • ROOT workspaces used for xRooFit comparisons.

Each benchmark documents the expected workspace format separately.

items.title: Benchmark Results
items.text:
results/

Every benchmark produces structured JSON output.

Results generated with the matrix runner follow the directory structure

results/

    <benchmark>/

        <workspace>/

            repeat_000/

                <benchmark>_result.json

This layout makes it possible to benchmark large collections of workspaces while keeping individual runs independent and reproducible.

The generated JSON files serve as the primary input for reporting and visualization.

items.title: Plots
items.text:
plots/

Generated figures are stored separately from numerical benchmark results.

Typical outputs include

  • execution time comparisons;
  • scaling plots;
  • memory usage;
  • cross-framework comparisons;
  • benchmark overview reports.

Separating plots from benchmark results allows figures to be regenerated without rerunning the benchmarks.

items.title: Documentation
items.text:
docs/

The documentation is organized into topic-oriented sections.

Major categories include

  • getting started;
  • benchmark methodology;
  • workflow benchmarks;
  • cross-framework benchmarks;
  • workspaces;
  • outputs;
  • development.

This organization keeps individual pages focused while avoiding an oversized README.

items.title: Profiling
items.text:
profiling/

The profiling directory contains utilities and experiments used to investigate performance bottlenecks beyond the standard benchmark suite.

These tools are primarily intended for development and optimization.

items.title: Tests
items.text:
tests/

Unit and integration tests validate benchmark correctness, workspace generation, and supporting utilities.

Where possible, benchmarks are validated against reference implementations to ensure numerical consistency.

items.title: Typical Benchmark Workflow
items.text:
A typical benchmark session follows the workflow below.

Input Workspace
        │
        ▼
Benchmark
        │
        ▼
JSON Result
        │
        ▼
Overview Builder
        │
        ▼
Plots
        │
        ▼
Documentation

The same workflow is used for both individual benchmark executions and large-scale matrix runs.

items.title: Matrix Runner
items.text:
The repository includes a unified matrix runner capable of executing benchmark suites over collections of workspaces.

Typical responsibilities include

  • discovering input workspaces;
  • executing benchmark suites;
  • collecting benchmark outputs;
  • handling repeated runs;
  • generating benchmark summaries.

Using a single runner ensures that all benchmarks follow the same execution model and produce results with a consistent directory structure.

items.title: Design Philosophy
items.text:
The repository is intentionally modular.

Each benchmark is responsible only for measuring one aspect of the statistical workflow.

Common functionality—including workspace discovery, benchmarking utilities, plotting infrastructure, and reporting—is shared across the project.

This separation simplifies maintenance, encourages reuse, and makes it straightforward to introduce new benchmark suites without modifying the existing architecture.

items.title: Benchmark Suite
items.text:
The PyHS3 Benchmarks repository provides a comprehensive collection of benchmarks covering the complete statistical model lifecycle.

Rather than focusing on a single operation, the benchmark suite evaluates every major stage involved in preparing, optimizing, and evaluating statistical models represented using the HS3 format.

All benchmark suites follow the common benchmarking methodology described in the Benchmark Methodology guide and operate on the canonical benchmark workspace collection unless stated otherwise.

items.title: Benchmark Categories
items.text: The benchmark suite is organized into three major categories.

items.title: Workflow Benchmarks
items.text:
Workflow benchmarks measure the cost of individual stages in the statistical model lifecycle.

These benchmarks isolate specific operations, making it possible to identify performance bottlenecks and evaluate optimization opportunities.

The available workflow benchmarks are

  • Workspace Loading
  • Model Creation
  • Log-Probability Construction
  • Log-Probability Compilation
  • Graph Canonicalization
  • Graph Optimization
  • Compiled Evaluation
  • PDF Evaluation
  • NLL Scan
  • Memory Scaling
  • Model Complexity Scaling

items.title: Cross-Framework Benchmarks
items.text:
Cross-framework benchmarks compare PyHS3 against other statistical inference frameworks.

These benchmarks evaluate both numerical agreement and execution performance.

Currently supported comparisons include

  • PyHS3 vs xRooFit
  • Scalar PDF Evaluation
  • Vectorized PDF Evaluation
  • Binned Likelihood Evaluation
  • NLL Scan
  • Model Complexity Scaling

items.title: Reporting
items.text:
Every benchmark produces

  • structured JSON reports;
  • optional visualization plots;
  • benchmark metadata;
  • validation information (where applicable).

Large benchmark campaigns additionally generate benchmark overview reports summarizing the entire execution.

items.title: Benchmark Pipeline
items.text:
The benchmark suite follows the statistical model lifecycle.

HS3 Workspace
      │
      ▼
Workspace Loading
      │
      ▼
Model Creation
      │
      ▼
Log-Probability Construction
      │
      ▼
Graph Canonicalization
      │
      ▼
Graph Optimization
      │
      ▼
Log-Probability Compilation
      │
      ▼
Compiled Evaluation
      │
      ├────────────► PDF Evaluation
      │
      ├────────────► NLL Scan
      │
      ├────────────► Memory Scaling
      │
      └────────────► Cross-Framework Benchmarks

The pipeline reflects the order in which statistical models are typically prepared and evaluated during analysis.

items.title: Workflow Benchmarks
items.text: Benchmark Purpose Workspace Loading Measure HS3 workspace deserialization Model Creation Measure statistical model construction Log-Probability Construction Build the computational graph Log-Probability Compilation Compile the graph for execution Graph Canonicalization Normalize computational graphs Graph Optimization Apply graph optimizations Compiled Evaluation Evaluate compiled likelihoods PDF Evaluation Measure probability density evaluation NLL Scan Scan the negative log-likelihood Memory Scaling Measure memory consumption Model Complexity Scaling Evaluate scalability across increasingly complex models

items.title: Cross-Framework Benchmarks
items.text: Benchmark Compared Frameworks PyHS3 vs xRooFit PyHS3, xRooFit Scalar PDF Evaluation PyHS3, RooFit, PyHF Vectorized PDF Evaluation PyHS3, RooFit Binned Likelihood Evaluation PyHS3, RooFit, PyHF NLL Scan PyHS3, RooFit Model Complexity Scaling Multiple frameworks

items.title: Running Benchmarks
items.text:
All benchmark suites can be executed using the matrix runner.

For example,

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks workspace_loading

or

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks all

Individual benchmark pages document benchmark-specific command-line options and generated outputs.

items.title: Related Documentation
items.text:
Workflow benchmark pages describe each benchmark in detail.

Cross-framework comparisons are documented separately.

Common benchmark execution principles are described in the Benchmark Methodology guide.

items.title: Benchmark Workspaces
items.text:
Benchmark workspaces define the statistical models used throughout the PyHS3 Benchmarks repository.

The repository is built around a single benchmark workspace collection that serves as the primary input for nearly every benchmark suite. Using a unified dataset ensures that performance measurements remain reproducible, directly comparable, and representative across the entire benchmarking framework.

Rather than maintaining separate benchmark datasets for different workflow stages, the repository uses the same workspace collection whenever possible. This allows benchmark results from different suites to be interpreted consistently and enables meaningful comparisons between workflow stages and statistical frameworks.

items.title: Workspace Collections
items.text: The repository currently contains two workspace collections.

items.title: Benchmark Workspaces
items.text:
The primary benchmark dataset.

These workspaces are used by virtually every workflow benchmark, scalability benchmark, and cross-framework benchmark implemented in the repository.

The collection spans a wide range of statistical models with varying complexity, including different numbers of channels, signal parameterizations, background models, nuisance parameter configurations, and constraint models.

See Benchmark Workspaces for a detailed description.

items.title: PyHF Workspaces
items.text:
The repository also contains benchmark inputs targeting the PyHF ecosystem.

These workspaces are documented separately because their structure and intended use differ from the primary benchmark workspace collection.

See PyHF Workspaces for additional details.

items.title: Why a Single Benchmark Dataset?
items.text:
Using one benchmark workspace collection throughout the repository provides several advantages.

  • consistent benchmark inputs across workflow stages;
  • reproducible performance measurements;
  • directly comparable benchmark results;
  • simplified benchmark maintenance;
  • identical statistical models for cross-framework validation.

As a result, benchmark differences reflect implementation differences rather than differences in benchmark inputs.

items.title: Matrix Benchmarking
items.text:
The matrix runner automatically discovers compatible benchmark workspaces and executes benchmark suites across the complete collection.

This enables large-scale benchmark campaigns with a single command while preserving a consistent directory structure for benchmark outputs.

items.title: Related Documentation
items.text:
• Benchmark Workspaces
  • PyHF Workspaces

items.title: Benchmark Workspaces
items.text:
The benchmark workspace collection is the primary dataset used throughout the PyHS3 Benchmarks repository.

Every workflow benchmark, scalability benchmark, and cross-framework benchmark operates on this collection unless stated otherwise.

Using a single benchmark dataset ensures that all benchmark results are directly comparable and eliminates variability caused by different statistical models.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
The collection consists of benchmark workspaces covering a broad range of statistical models.

The workspaces vary in

  • number of channels;
  • background parameterization;
  • signal model;
  • signal shape configuration;
  • nuisance parameter configuration;
  • constraint model;
  • signal yield.

Together these configurations provide benchmark inputs spanning a wide spectrum of statistical complexity.

items.title: Workspace Naming Convention
items.text:
Each workspace filename completely describes the statistical model it contains.

The naming convention follows the grammar

<channels>_<background>_<signal>_<shape>_<nuisance>_<constraints>_<yield>

For example,

10ch_bkgGenExp_sigGeneric_shapeFloat_npOn_constrGauss_yield1x

can be interpreted as

Component Description 10ch Ten analysis channels bkgGenExp Generated exponential background model sigGeneric Generic signal model shapeFloat Floating signal-shape parameters npOn Nuisance parameters enabled constrGauss Gaussian constraints yield1x Nominal signal yield

This naming scheme makes it possible to understand the model configuration directly from the filename without inspecting the workspace itself.

items.title: Workspace Complexity
items.text:
The collection contains models of varying complexity.

Typical channel counts include

  • 1 channel;
  • 3 channels;
  • 10 channels;
  • 15 channels;
  • 20 channels;
  • 25 channels;
  • 30 channels.

Increasing the number of channels increases the overall model complexity and is therefore particularly useful for scalability studies.

items.title: ROOT Counterparts
items.text:
Each benchmark workspace has a corresponding ROOT workspace representing the same statistical model.

These ROOT workspaces are used for cross-framework benchmarks comparing PyHS3 with xRooFit.

Using equivalent statistical models across frameworks ensures that observed differences originate from implementation rather than model definition.

items.title: Benchmark Coverage
items.text:
The benchmark workspace collection is used throughout the repository.

Benchmark Suite Uses Benchmark Workspaces Workflow Benchmarks ✅ Scaling Benchmarks ✅ Memory Benchmarks ✅ Compiled Evaluation ✅ NLL Scan ✅ Cross-Framework Benchmarks ✅ PyHS3 vs xRooFit ✅

items.title: Automatic Discovery
items.text:
Benchmark workspaces are automatically discovered by the matrix runner.

Executing

pixi run python -m src.run_alexx_workspace_benchmark_matrix \
    --workspace-dir inputs/alexx_workspaces \
    --benchmarks all

runs every supported benchmark over the complete benchmark workspace collection.

Manual benchmark registration is therefore unnecessary when using the standard repository layout.

items.title: Design Philosophy
items.text:
The benchmark workspace collection serves as the canonical benchmark dataset for the repository.

Using a single, well-defined collection greatly simplifies benchmarking, enables reproducible performance evaluation, and provides a common reference point for workflow benchmarks, scalability studies, and cross-framework comparisons.

New benchmark suites are therefore encouraged to operate on this collection whenever possible.
